verdict_reasoning: ABCB9 is a lysosomal ABC transporter with demonstrated exclusive localization to lysosomes via direct ER-to-Golgi-to-early endosome-to-lysosome trafficking. Surface biotinylation and RUSH-synchronized trafficking assays show NO plasma membrane (PM) residency at baseline. The N-terminal TMD0 domain actively directs lysosomal targeting and prevents default PM trafficking—truncation of TMD0 causes aberrant PM misdirection but this is an artifact of deletion, not a native isoform. No documented cell-state-dependent surfacing (stress, activation, infection, differentiation) exists. No therapeutic antibodies, CAR-Ts, ADCs, or bispecifics target ABCB9 on intact cells (the soluble peptide-transport function is lysosomal-lumen-facing). Contextual buckets ruled out: (1) cell_state_induced—no evidence; (2) tissue_restricted_surface—high expression in testis Sertoli cells but localization is still lysosomal, not PM; (3) lysosomal_exocytosis—TAPL is a lysosomal membrane protein but lacks evidence of stimulus-induced exocytosis; (4) dual_localization—truncation studies show PM is a default misrouting fate when TMD0 is absent, not a native steady-state or dynamic pool; (5) stable_surface_attachment—not secreted.
user_message:
Triage the human gene **ABCB9**.

Canonical identifiers and gene summary (machine-resolved from HGNC and NCBI; no further lookups available — judge from the context below plus your trained knowledge):

- HGNC approved name: ATP binding cassette subfamily B member 9
- HGNC symbol: ABCB9
- UniProt accession: Q9NP78
- Aliases: EST122234, TAPL
- Previous symbols: (none)
- HGNC gene-group memberships: ATP binding cassette subfamily B
- CD nomenclature: (none assigned)
- NCBI summary: The membrane-associated protein encoded by this gene is a member of the superfamily of ATP-binding cassette (ABC) transporters. ABC proteins transport various molecules across extra- and intra-cellular membranes. ABC genes are divided into seven distinct subfamilies (ABC1, MDR/TAP, MRP, ALD, OABP, GCN20, White). This protein is a member of the MDR/TAP subfamily. Members of the MDR/TAP subfamily are involved in multidrug resistance as well as antigen presentation. This family member functions in the translocation of peptides from the cytosol into the lysosomal lumen. Alternative splicing of th…
